pattern: Tracking, round by round, Bob's previous creature together with the running point difference lets a dynamic program count every no-repeat sequence that ends up ahead, since each round's outcome against Alice's fixed move is fully determined by Bob's two eligible choices.
hints: Every round's outcome only depends on Alice's fixed creature for that round and Bob's chosen creature, so it always resolves to Bob gaining a point, Alice gaining a point, or neither -- a fixed three-way rule.

Bob's only restriction is that his creature must differ from the one he used the previous round, so a state tracking his last creature plus the running point difference (Bob's points minus Alice's) can transition round by round.
After processing every round, summing the counts over every previous-creature state where the running difference is strictly positive gives exactly the sequences where Bob ends up ahead.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, Alice always plays Fire Dragon
statement example, a mixed five-round sequence
Alice cycles through all three creatures repeatedly
Alice always plays the same creature over many rounds
two rounds, checking the smallest case with a real choice

stress.description: random creature sequences at small-to-moderate length to exercise the score-difference dynamic program
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 60))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('FWE') for _ in range(n))
    return [s]

stress.description: longer random sequences approaching the upper length bound
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 400))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('FWE') for _ in range(n))
    return [s]
